item.title: Multiple mineralization events in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt: Insights from geology, geochronology, and geochemistry of the giant Tongshan porphyry Cu deposit, NE China
item.dc:creator: Chenglin Bai
Guiqing Xie
Junkang Zhao
Tianqi Wang
Jie Chen
item.description: The Tongshan deposit is a giant porphyry Cu system in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt where complex alteration and mineralization processes have remained poorly constrained. This study elucidated its evolution through integrated field geology, Re-Os geochronology, and sulfur isotope analysis. Mineralization in the western segment of Tongshan is hosted in granodiorite porphyry and tonalite and is associated with phyllic and localized potassic alteration. In the eastern segment, mineralization occurs within Ordovician Duobaoshan Formation volcanic rocks and the roof pendants of granodiorite and dacite porphyry, where propylitic alteration overprints earlier alteration assemblages. Two distinct mineralization episodes were identified: an initial porphyry-style mineralization (episode A) and a later overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization (episode B). Episode A comprises three stages (A-I, A-II, and A-III), with the main Cu mineralization in stage A-II forming quartz-chalcopyrite ± molybdenite ± pyrite veinlets with K-feldspar halos. Episode B veins overprinted earlier veins and contributed significant additional Cu through four stages (B-I, B-II, B-III, and B-IV) of wider quartz-sulfide veining. Systematic logging showed a positive correlation between the density of overprinting veins and Cu(-Mo-Au) grades. Molybdenite Re-Os dating suggests that episode A and B veins likely occurred at 475.6 ± 2.3 Ma and 228.1 ± 2.9 Ma, respectively. These ages suggest that the porphyry-style mineralization corresponds to the Ordovician granodiorite porphyry intrusions, whereas the overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization was associated with Triassic granodiorite and dacite porphyry. A significant characteristic at Tongshan is the prolonged interval of more than 200 m.y. between the two mineralization episodes. The δ34S values of the episode A sulfides range from −2.1‰ to −0.5‰, which is consistent with a magmatic source. The episode B sulfide δ34S signatures of −5.6‰ to +0.9‰ are analogous to those of a mantle-crust mixed source, indicating the Duobaoshan Formation may have been a potential sulfur reservoir. These results provide a refined genetic model for Tongshan and valuable insights for exploring other Paleozoic porphyry Cu deposits in the Central Asian orogenic belt. Published: 2026-07-09T21:55:56+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.635 (must_read)

item.title: An early jurassic gold mineralization event in the Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern margin of the North China Craton: evidence from two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite in the Linjia deposit
item.dc:creator: Wanyi Feng
Jiahao Zheng
Bin Chen
Shuaijie Liu
item.description: Apatite is a widespread accessory mineral that occurs frequently in magmatic rocks and diverse hydrothermal ore deposits, and its textural features, geochronology, and geochemical signatures offer critical constraints on magmatic activity and metasomatic events. Here, two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite were identified by CL imaging in the hydrothermal altered auriferous granite in the Linjia gold deposit, Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern part of the North China Craton (NCC). The magmatic apatite (Ap1) grains with bright CL imaging formed earlier than the hydrothermal apatite (Ap2) crystals with dark CL imaging. The U-Pb dating results suggest the Ap1 formed contemporaneously with magmatic zircon in the granite at ca. 231 Ma, and these Triassic dates define the emplacement age of the ore-hosting granite. By contrast, the Ap2 and hydrothermal rutiles in the mineralized granite and schist both formed at ca. 186 Ma. The hydrothermal Ap2 grains show much higher S contents than the Ap1, suggesting they may be precipitated contemporaneously with sulfides from the S-bearing hydrothermal fluids. In addition, the calculated δ18Ofluid values, relatively high U as well as Th contents, and REE pattern of Ap2 are clearly distinct from metamorphic fluids, but comparable to magmatic‑hydrothermal fluids derived from granitic magmas. The textural, geochemical, and isotopic evidence indicates that Ap2 and rutiles formed from hydrothermal fluids during gold precipitation, and their Early Jurassic U-Pb ages represent the timing of gold mineralization. Based on the above evidence, we infer that Linjia represents an intrusion-related gold deposit related to a buried Early Jurassic felsic intrusion at depth. Importantly, the newly identified gold mineralization in the Linjia deposit coincides with the onset of paleo-Pacific plate subduction beneath the NCC, suggesting that there was a relatively small-scale Early Jurassic gold mineralization related to subduction prior to the ca. 120 Ma large-scale gold accumulation event in the NCC. This study highlights the importance of combining the microtextures, geochronological data, trace elements, and isotopic compositions of individual apatite grains to unravel the genesis of cryptic magmatic and hydrothermal apatite and reconstruct obscure geological events within magmatic-hydrothermal systems. Published: 2026-07-08T07:06:01+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.630 (consider)

In addition, the calculated δ18Ofluid values, relatively high U as well as Th contents, and REE pattern of Ap2 are clearly distinct from metamorphic fluids, but comparable to magmatic‑hydrothermal fluids derived from granitic magmas. The textural, geochemical, and isotopic evidence indicates that Ap2 and rutiles formed from hydrothermal fluids during gold precipitation, and their Early Jurassic U-Pb ages represent the timing of gold mineralization. Based on the above evidence, we infer that Linjia represents an intrusion-related gold deposit related to a buried Early Jurassic felsic intrusion at depth. Importantly, the newly identified gold mineralization in the Linjia deposit coincides with the onset of paleo-Pacific plate subduction beneath the NCC, suggesting that there was a relatively small-scale Early Jurassic gold mineralization related to subduction prior to the ca. 120 Ma large-scale gold accumulation event in the NCC. This study highlights the importance of combining the microtextures, geochronological data, trace elements, and isotopic compositions of individual apatite grains to unravel the genesis of cryptic magmatic and hydrothermal apatite and reconstruct obscure geological events within magmatic-hydrothermal systems.

item.title: Paleoproterozoic Dongyaozhuang orogenic gold deposit in the Trans-North China Orogen: Constraints from in-situ U-Th-Pb dating of hydrothermal monazite and trace elements and sulfur isotopes of sulfides
item.dc:creator: Ying Ma
Shao-Yong Jiang
Ning Jin
Dao-Hui Pi
Yu Jiang
Guo-Hai Guo
item.description: The Wutai Complex in the Trans-North China Orogen hosts several significant Precambrian mesothermal, greenstone-hosted gold deposits. However, as in many Precambrian orogenic belts worldwide, these deposits are associated with complex hydrothermal systems involving multistage fluid activity during successive mineralization stages. As a result, their age, origin, evolutionary history, and genetic mechanisms remain poorly constrained. Here, we present in-situ hydrothermal monazite U-Th-Pb geochronological data, together with trace-element and S isotope analyses of multiple generations of pyrite and arsenopyrite, from the representative greenstone-hosted Dongyaozhuang gold deposit in the Wutai Complex. These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks. Published: 2026-07-04T01:02:54+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.630 (consider)

The Wutai Complex in the Trans-North China Orogen hosts several significant Precambrian mesothermal, greenstone-hosted gold deposits. However, as in many Precambrian orogenic belts worldwide, these deposits are associated with complex hydrothermal systems involving multistage fluid activity during successive mineralization stages. As a result, their age, origin, evolutionary history, and genetic mechanisms remain poorly constrained. Here, we present in-situ hydrothermal monazite U-Th-Pb geochronological data, together with trace-element and S isotope analyses of multiple generations of pyrite and arsenopyrite, from the representative greenstone-hosted Dongyaozhuang gold deposit in the Wutai Complex. These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks.

item.title: Evolution of Sulfur Concentration in the Deep Earth During the Proto‐Earth Growth: First‐Principles Study on the Sulfur Partitioning Between Metal and Silicate at High Pressure and Temperature
item.dc:creator: K. Itoh
T. Tsuchiya
item.description: Abstract Sulfur (S) is one of the promising light elements that can explain the density deficit of the core. The degree of siderophile nature of S under high pressure and temperature ( P , T ) is the key to estimating the S content in the core. However, a significant discrepancy between the metal‐silicate partition coefficient of S extrapolated from relatively low‐ P and that measured at deep mantle P , T is identified. In this study, we calculate the partition behavior of S between liquid iron and molten silicate in a wide range of P , T conditions using the ab initio thermodynamic integration molecular dynamics approach. The results indicate that S is strongly siderophile at 0–135 GPa and 3,000–5,000K, but the partition coefficient of S () markedly decreases with increasing the oxygen content in liquid iron. The pressure dependence of is also found to change around 20 GPa. These properties might reconcile the experimental conflicts and can be interpreted based on the atomic structures of the liquids. Using the derived , we model the evolution of the S concentrations in the core and mantle during the growth of the proto‐Earth. According to this model, the S concentration retained in the mantle increases as the size of the accreting planetesimals decreases and as the core becomes more oxidized. Our results further indicate that S could be a major light element in the core when the S concentration in the accreting planetesimals is larger than ∼3 mol%. Published: 2026-07-08T10:57:17+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.627 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: K. Itoh; T. Tsuchiya

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2026gc013030

Relevance: 0.627 (consider)

Abstract Sulfur (S) is one of the promising light elements that can explain the density deficit of the core. The degree of siderophile nature of S under high pressure and temperature ( P , T ) is the key to estimating the S content in the core. However, a significant discrepancy between the metal‐silicate partition coefficient of S extrapolated from relatively low‐ P and that measured at deep mantle P , T is identified. In this study, we calculate the partition behavior of S between liquid iron and molten silicate in a wide range of P , T conditions using the ab initio thermodynamic integration molecular dynamics approach. The results indicate that S is strongly siderophile at 0–135 GPa and 3,000–5,000K, but the partition coefficient of S () markedly decreases with increasing the oxygen content in liquid iron. The pressure dependence of is also found to change around 20 GPa. These properties might reconcile the experimental conflicts and can be interpreted based on the atomic structures of the liquids. Using the derived , we model the evolution of the S concentrations in the core and mantle during the growth of the proto‐Earth. According to this model, the S concentration retained in the mantle increases as the size of the accreting planetesimals decreases and as the core becomes more oxidized. Our results further indicate that S could be a major light element in the core when the S concentration in the accreting planetesimals is larger than ∼3 mol%.

item.title: Multiple Au enrichment processes in the Early Cretaceous Heilongtan–Xiejiagou deposit, Tongbai Orogen, Central China
item.dc:creator: Jun-Jie Zhou
Zhan-Ke Li
Mei-Zhen Yang
Da Ke
Shao-Rui Zhao
Ke-Qing Zong
Zaicong Wang
Bo Zu
Le-Le Ding
item.description: The Tongbai Orogen is an important Au province in central China, but the processes responsible for localized high-grade Au enrichment in its southern segment remain poorly constrained. Here, we investigate the Heilongtan–Xiejiagou Au deposit using ore geology, hand-specimen-scale element mapping, ore-stage geochronology, and in-situ trace-element and S–Pb isotope analyses of sulfides. The deposit records three stages of mineralization: Stage I quartz–sericite–pyrite alteration, Stage II quartz–pyrite vein, and Stage III quartz–polymetallic sulfide vein. Ore-stage geochronology based on Rb-Sr and U-Pb isotopic systems of Stage I sericite and monazite, and Stage III muscovite constrains the main Au–Ag mineralization to ca. 140–134 Ma. The S–Pb isotope data, together with the Early Cretaceous ages and spatial association with granitoid magmatism, indicate a major magmatic–hydrothermal contribution, with variable input from Neoproterozoic host rocks. Pyrite records a systematic evolution from As-rich invisible-Au-bearing Py-I, through porous and inclusion-rich Py-II associated with Au redistribution and polymetallic enrichment, to trace-element-poor Py-III associated with late polymetallic sulfides. These features indicate that Stage II was the main stage of Au remobilization and local reconcentration. During this stage, coupled dissolution–reprecipitation of earlier auriferous pyrite, together with additional Au input from the infiltrating fluid, generated porous reaction domains that promoted Au release, redistribution, and electrum precipitation. These results link the deposit to Early Cretaceous magmatic–hydrothermal activity and suggest that exploration in the southern Tongbai Orogen should focus on reactivated vein systems capable of localized high-grade upgrading. Overall, this study provides new insight into fluid-mediated remobilization and local reconcentration of pyrite-hosted Au in high-grade lode gold systems. Published: 2026-07-03T23:08:06+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.620 (consider)

Here, we investigate the Heilongtan–Xiejiagou Au deposit using ore geology, hand-specimen-scale element mapping, ore-stage geochronology, and in-situ trace-element and S–Pb isotope analyses of sulfides. The deposit records three stages of mineralization: Stage I quartz–sericite–pyrite alteration, Stage II quartz–pyrite vein, and Stage III quartz–polymetallic sulfide vein. Ore-stage geochronology based on Rb-Sr and U-Pb isotopic systems of Stage I sericite and monazite, and Stage III muscovite constrains the main Au–Ag mineralization to ca. 140–134 Ma. The S–Pb isotope data, together with the Early Cretaceous ages and spatial association with granitoid magmatism, indicate a major magmatic–hydrothermal contribution, with variable input from Neoproterozoic host rocks. Pyrite records a systematic evolution from As-rich invisible-Au-bearing Py-I, through porous and inclusion-rich Py-II associated with Au redistribution and polymetallic enrichment, to trace-element-poor Py-III associated with late polymetallic sulfides. These features indicate that Stage II was the main stage of Au remobilization and local reconcentration. During this stage, coupled dissolution–reprecipitation of earlier auriferous pyrite, together with additional Au input from the infiltrating fluid, generated porous reaction domains that promoted Au release, redistribution, and electrum precipitation. These results link the deposit to Early Cretaceous magmatic–hydrothermal activity and suggest that exploration in the southern Tongbai Orogen should focus on reactivated vein systems capable of localized high-grade upgrading. Overall, this study provides new insight into fluid-mediated remobilization and local reconcentration of pyrite-hosted Au in high-grade lode gold systems.

item.title: Gold mineralisation characteristics of the typical ore body and the potential for exploration in the Qiucun Au deposit, Dehua county, Fujian province, China
item.dc:creator: Fei-Peng Fan
Pei Ni
Fan Xiao
Yan Zhou
Guang-Fu Xing
Zi-Hao Zhao
Zhi-Qiang Yu
Ming-Seng Fan
Nan Wang
item.description: The Qiucun Au deposit is located in Dehua County, Fujian Province, within the Wuyi Mountain metallogenic belt. Gold mineralisation of the typical ore body exhibits distinct zoning characteristics between 940 m and 647 m, is structurally controlled by a volcanic conduit and its associated fracture zones, and is closely associated with quartz (chalcedony)-sericite-pyrite-calcite assemblages. To decipher the ore-forming processes of the Qiucun deposit, detailed geological investigations were carried out, along with sulfur isotope analysis of syn-ore pyrite, microthermometric analysis (homogenization temperature and salinity) of fluid inclusions in syn-ore quartz, and U-Pb dating of syn-ore calcite. The results show that in typical orebodies, gold grade increases and then decreases from shallow to deeper sections. Gold occurs mainly as fracture-fillings within pyrite, displaying diverse morphologies and colours that correlate with grade. Significant gold mineralisation is hosted within sericite alteration zones, whereas the presence of calcite is generally associated with lower gold grades. The δ 34 S values of gold-related pyrite range from –6.8‰ to + 1.6‰, suggesting a deep magmatic source for the ore-forming materials. Fluid inclusions in quartz are dominated by two-phase (liquid-rich) types with minor evidence of boiling assemblages, obtaining low-temperature (180–260 °C) and low-salinity (0.35–5.56 wt% NaCl equiv.). U-Pb dating of calcite yields an age of 152.0 ± 17 Ma. Integrated evidence indicates that the Qiucun low-sulfidation epithermal Au deposit is related to the subduction of the paleo-Pacific Plate. Fractured volcanic breccia zones served as the main pathways for hydrothermal fluid migration and ore precipitation. Pyrite, sericite, calcite, and other minerals at different elevations are the main indicators for further gold exploration in the Qiucun gold deposit and its adjacent areas. Published: 2026-07-06T23:29:31+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.620 (consider)

Fractured volcanic breccia zones served as the main pathways for hydrothermal fluid migration and ore precipitation. Pyrite, sericite, calcite, and other minerals at different elevations are the main indicators for further gold exploration in the Qiucun gold deposit and its adjacent areas.

item.title: Sulfur and lead isotope geochemistry of sulfide minerals from the Yushui copper deposit, eastern Guangdong, South China
item.dc:creator: Gang Chen
Changhui Ke
Junzeng Zuo
Zhihui Dai
Maohong Chen
item.description: The Yushui volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS) copper deposit in eastern Guangdong, South China, is distinguished by two discrete mineralization events: a Carboniferous stratiform ore system (ca. 308 Ma) and a Jurassic vein-type overprint (ca. 163 Ma). In situ sulfur and lead isotope analyses (LA‑MC‑ICP‑MS) were conducted on sulfides from both ore types to constrain sulfur and metal sources and hydrothermal system evolution. Sulfur isotopes (δ34S = − 23.88‰ to + 4.01‰) show systematic variations: early exhalative and stockwork ores record significant bacterial and thermochemical reduction of seawater sulfate, whereas main‑stage massive and banded ores approach magmatic sulfur values (0‰ to + 3‰). Vein‑type ores yield δ34S indicative of a dominant magmatic source linked to Jurassic igneous activity. Lead isotopes form three linear groups corresponding to Carboniferous VMS mineralization, Jurassic vein‑type overprint, and host volcanic rocks. Lead in stratiform ores was largely leached from basement crust with minor magmatic input, while vein‑type ores predominantly inherited lead from pre‑existing stratiform ores in a closed system. These results confirm a two‑stage genesis: (1) shallow‑marine seafloor hydrothermal sedimentation forming primary VMS ore bodies at ∼ 308 Ma; (2) Jurassic magmatic‑hydrothermal reworking and superimposition generating vein‑type ore bodies at ∼ 163 Ma. This study highlights the utility of in situ S–Pb isotopes in unravelling polyphase ore‑forming processes in VMS deposits modified by later tectono‑magmatic events. Published: 2026-07-08T07:06:10+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.615 (consider)

In situ sulfur and lead isotope analyses (LA‑MC‑ICP‑MS) were conducted on sulfides from both ore types to constrain sulfur and metal sources and hydrothermal system evolution. Sulfur isotopes (δ34S = − 23.88‰ to + 4.01‰) show systematic variations: early exhalative and stockwork ores record significant bacterial and thermochemical reduction of seawater sulfate, whereas main‑stage massive and banded ores approach magmatic sulfur values (0‰ to + 3‰). Vein‑type ores yield δ34S indicative of a dominant magmatic source linked to Jurassic igneous activity. Lead isotopes form three linear groups corresponding to Carboniferous VMS mineralization, Jurassic vein‑type overprint, and host volcanic rocks. Lead in stratiform ores was largely leached from basement crust with minor magmatic input, while vein‑type ores predominantly inherited lead from pre‑existing stratiform ores in a closed system. These results confirm a two‑stage genesis: (1) shallow‑marine seafloor hydrothermal sedimentation forming primary VMS ore bodies at ∼ 308 Ma; (2) Jurassic magmatic‑hydrothermal reworking and superimposition generating vein‑type ore bodies at ∼ 163 Ma. This study highlights the utility of in situ S–Pb isotopes in unravelling polyphase ore‑forming processes in VMS deposits modified by later tectono‑magmatic events.

item.title: Niobium and heavy rare earth element mineralization in the Bayan Obo deposit, China: insights from aeschynite mineralogy, geochemistry, and geochronology
item.dc:creator: Qing Sun
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content. Published: 2026-07-04T22:23:19+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.615 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qing Sun; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102405

Relevance: 0.615 (consider)

The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content.

item.title: Tracing the Origin and Hydrothermal Overprint of Magnetite-Apatite Mineralization in the New Afton Cu-Au ± PGE Alkalic Porphyry Deposit: Insights from Magnetite Textures and Chemistry
item.dc:creator: Andres Felipe Gonzalez-Duran
Adam C. Simon
Robert Holder
John F.H. Thompson
Devin Wade
Catherine Ryan
Ryan Mathur
item.description: Abstract Alkalic porphyry deposits are significant sources of Cu-Au ± platinum group elements (PGE), with magnetite being particularly abundant in the Triassic alkalic systems of British Columbia, Canada. The New Afton area hosts both Cu-Au ± PGE mineralization and several massive magnetite lodes, which were historically mined for iron. The origin of these magnetite orebodies and their relationship to alkalic porphyry mineralization remains unclear. This study integrates field observations and magnetite chemistry and textures to elucidate their formation. We identify two stages of magnetite mineralization: an early high-temperature (>500°C) magmatic-hydrothermal stage, characterized by ilmenite exsolution lamellae, high-Ti magnetite, and symplectitic intergrowths with ilmenite, followed by a later stage of hydrothermal reequilibration that precipitated ilmenite-free Ti-depleted magnetite. Rather than preserving its original crystallization signature, magnetite records a complex history of hydrothermal activity, including Cu-Au-PGE-bearing magmatic-hydrothermal fluid circulation and repeated, superimposed coupled dissolution-reprecipitation events. These findings align with those from other magmatic-hydrothermal ore systems such as iron oxide copper-gold and Kiruna-type iron oxide-apatite deposits, emphasizing the widespread nature of magnetite reequilibration and the need for caution when using magnetite as a petrogenetic indicator. Published: 2026-07-08T19:38:19+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.600 (consider)

The origin of these magnetite orebodies and their relationship to alkalic porphyry mineralization remains unclear. We identify two stages of magnetite mineralization: an early high-temperature (>500°C) magmatic-hydrothermal stage, characterized by ilmenite exsolution lamellae, high-Ti magnetite, and symplectitic intergrowths with ilmenite, followed by a later stage of hydrothermal reequilibration that precipitated ilmenite-free Ti-depleted magnetite. Rather than preserving its original crystallization signature, magnetite records a complex history of hydrothermal activity, including Cu-Au-PGE-bearing magmatic-hydrothermal fluid circulation and repeated, superimposed coupled dissolution-reprecipitation events. These findings align with those from other magmatic-hydrothermal ore systems such as iron oxide copper-gold and Kiruna-type iron oxide-apatite deposits, emphasizing the widespread nature of magnetite reequilibration and the need for caution when using magnetite as a petrogenetic indicator.

item.title: Repeated recharge of primitive melts in the lower oceanic crust: Constraints from the Dongco ophiolite, Central Tibet
item.dc:creator: Xiao-Ni Li
Chuan-Zhou Liu
Wei-Qi Zhang
Tong Liu
Chang Zhang
Yin-Zheng Lin
Zhen Zhang
item.description: Magma recharge and associated melt–mush interaction can substantially modify the composition of the lower oceanic crust. However, the mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of such interaction remain poorly constrained. Here, we present petrological, geochronological, and geochemical data for poikilitic olivine gabbros, layered olivine gabbros and layered gabbros from the Dongco ophiolite in central Tibet. Zircon U-Pb ages and mineral Sr-Nd isotopes indicate that the poikilitic olivine gabbros and their host layered gabbros were coeval and derived from the same magmatic system. Both the poikilitic olivine gabbros and the layered olivine gabbros record evidence of melt–mush interaction. The poikilitic olivine gabbros display clear disequilibrium textures, including clinopyroxene oikocrysts enclosing plagioclase chadacrysts and vermicular orthopyroxene surrounding olivine grains. The clinopyroxene oikocrysts exhibit oscillatory and normal Cr zoning from core to rim. In contrast, the layered olivine gabbros lack conspicuous reaction textures, whereas clinopyroxene in these rocks preserves oscillatory, normal, and reverse Cr zoning, indicating cryptic melt–mush interaction. These textural and mineral-chemical features indicate that both the poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros recorded melt–mush interaction, but at different levels of textural expression. In addition, clinopyroxene grains from both poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros are characterized by low TiO2 contents and the absence of significant core-to-rim variations in incompatible trace elements, suggesting that the reacting melt was primitive rather than evolved. Assimilation-fractionation-crystallization modelling further indicates that clinopyroxene oikocrysts in the poikilitic olivine gabbros formed under melt-dominated reaction conditions, with limited assimilation of pre-existing mush components, consistent with their coarse grain sizes. In contrast, clinopyroxene in the layered olivine gabbros records a larger contribution from assimilated or reacted mush components relative to crystallization, indicating stronger mush-buffered modification. These results demonstrate that repeated recharge of primitive melts can modify mineral compositions and geochemical signatures of gabbroic cumulates, thereby contributing to the chemical evolution of the lower oceanic crust. item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiao-Ni Li; Chuan-Zhou Liu; Wei-Qi Zhang; Tong Liu; Chang Zhang; Yin-Zheng Lin; Zhen Zhang

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108709

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

Magma recharge and associated melt–mush interaction can substantially modify the composition of the lower oceanic crust. However, the mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of such interaction remain poorly constrained. Here, we present petrological, geochronological, and geochemical data for poikilitic olivine gabbros, layered olivine gabbros and layered gabbros from the Dongco ophiolite in central Tibet. Zircon U-Pb ages and mineral Sr-Nd isotopes indicate that the poikilitic olivine gabbros and their host layered gabbros were coeval and derived from the same magmatic system. Both the poikilitic olivine gabbros and the layered olivine gabbros record evidence of melt–mush interaction. The poikilitic olivine gabbros display clear disequilibrium textures, including clinopyroxene oikocrysts enclosing plagioclase chadacrysts and vermicular orthopyroxene surrounding olivine grains. The clinopyroxene oikocrysts exhibit oscillatory and normal Cr zoning from core to rim. In contrast, the layered olivine gabbros lack conspicuous reaction textures, whereas clinopyroxene in these rocks preserves oscillatory, normal, and reverse Cr zoning, indicating cryptic melt–mush interaction. These textural and mineral-chemical features indicate that both the poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros recorded melt–mush interaction, but at different levels of textural expression. In addition, clinopyroxene grains from both poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros are characterized by low TiO2 contents and the absence of significant core-to-rim variations in incompatible trace elements, suggesting that the reacting melt was primitive rather than evolved. Assimilation-fractionation-crystallization modelling further indicates that clinopyroxene oikocrysts in the poikilitic olivine gabbros formed under melt-dominated reaction conditions, with limited assimilation of pre-existing mush components, consistent with their coarse grain sizes. In contrast, clinopyroxene in the layered olivine gabbros records a larger contribution from assimilated or reacted mush components relative to crystallization, indicating stronger mush-buffered modification. These results demonstrate that repeated recharge of primitive melts can modify mineral compositions and geochemical signatures of gabbroic cumulates, thereby contributing to the chemical evolution of the lower oceanic crust.

item.title: Devonian Jinshuikou pegmatites at the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau: UPb and SmNd constraints on their genetic relationship with regional granitoids
item.dc:creator: Si-Qi Liu
Shao-Yong Jiang
Mostafa Fayek
Hui-Min Su
Yu-Ying Che
Wei Wang
Qinglin Xia
item.description: Granitic pegmatites represent critical repositories of strategic rare metals (e.g., Li, Be, Nb, Ta) and are conventionally interpreted as terminal products of highly evolved granitic magmas. However, this classic genetic model was frequently challenged where pronounced age gaps exist between pegmatites and their putative host granitoids. Along the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau lies a world-class lithium metallogenic belt hosting approximately 14.8 million tons of Li2O resources. As a key segment of this belt, the East Kunlun Orogen contains widespread granitoids and newly recognized pegmatite dikes in the Jinshuikou area, yet their emplacement ages and genetic linkage between each other remain poorly constrained. Here we present integrated in situ UPb geochronology, mineral chemistry, and SmNd isotope data for monazite, garnet, and columbite group minerals (CGMs) from the pegmatites to resolve these issues. UPb dating of monazite and CGMs yields consistent ages of 372–375 Ma, firmly establishing a Devonian crystallization age for the pegmatites. Despite a ~ 15 Myr age difference relative to the youngest coeval Devonian granitoids (ca. 389 Ma), SmNd isotopic systematics strongly support a genetic connection between them. Monazite from pegmatites yields a SmNd isochron age of 390.2 ± 6.0 Ma and εNd(t) values (−6.01 to −5.39) identical to those of coeval I-type granitoids in the region. These data indicate that the pegmatites and associated granitoids originated from a common deep crustal magma reservoir generated by partial melting of ancient continental crust with limited input from juvenile mantle materials. The pegmatites represent the final products of protracted magmatic evolution within a long-lived crustal magma chamber. This study not only documents a previously overlooked Paleozoic rare-metal pegmatite system in the East Kunlun Orogen but also demonstrates that age gaps alone do not negate a cogenetic relationship between pegmatites and regional granitoids, with important implications for rare-metal exploration models in orogenic belts. Published: 2026-07-06T23:28:42+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.600 (consider)

Granitic pegmatites represent critical repositories of strategic rare metals (e.g., Li, Be, Nb, Ta) and are conventionally interpreted as terminal products of highly evolved granitic magmas. However, this classic genetic model was frequently challenged where pronounced age gaps exist between pegmatites and their putative host granitoids. Here we present integrated in situ UPb geochronology, mineral chemistry, and SmNd isotope data for monazite, garnet, and columbite group minerals (CGMs) from the pegmatites to resolve these issues. UPb dating of monazite and CGMs yields consistent ages of 372–375 Ma, firmly establishing a Devonian crystallization age for the pegmatites. Despite a ~ 15 Myr age difference relative to the youngest coeval Devonian granitoids (ca. 389 Ma), SmNd isotopic systematics strongly support a genetic connection between them. Monazite from pegmatites yields a SmNd isochron age of 390.2 ± 6.0 Ma and εNd(t) values (−6.01 to −5.39) identical to those of coeval I-type granitoids in the region. These data indicate that the pegmatites and associated granitoids originated from a common deep crustal magma reservoir generated by partial melting of ancient continental crust with limited input from juvenile mantle materials. The pegmatites represent the final products of protracted magmatic evolution within a long-lived crustal magma chamber. This study not only documents a previously overlooked Paleozoic rare-metal pegmatite system in the East Kunlun Orogen but also demonstrates that age gaps alone do not negate a cogenetic relationship between pegmatites and regional granitoids, with important implications for rare-metal exploration models in orogenic belts.

item.title: Linking Himalayan uplift to crustal deformation: Structural and multi-chronometer constraints from the Kangmar dome
item.dc:creator: Zhen Li
Pengfei Li
Dewen Zheng
Fred Jourdan
Gideon Rosenbaum
Qiang Wang
item.description: The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. To elucidate the crustal processes that accompanied the rise of the Himalayas, we investigated the deformational history of the Kangmar dome in the eastern Himalayas. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history. Published: 2026-07-05T21:54:24+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)

The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history.

item.title: Structurally Controlled Deposition of Gold During Inversion of Sedimentary Basin: A Case Study of Sediment-Hosted Orogenic Gold in the Gaolong Deposit, Youjiang Basin, South China Block
item.dc:creator: Yushan Zeng
Lin Yang
Qingfei Wang
Jun Deng
Artur P. Deditius
Jian-Xin Zhao
Ting Cheng
Shiyu Zhao
Shicai Huang
Di Ruan
item.description: Abstract The Youjiang basin underwent late Paleozoic expansion and Triassic inversion, resulting in scattered Paleozoic isolated carbonate platforms surrounded by Triassic turbidites. The Gaolong gold deposit is representative of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits formed along isolated platforms in the central-southern basin, where three stages of deformation were identified. The pre-ore D1 deformation in Gaolong features a WNW-trending brachy-anticline and NNE-trending dextral strike-slip shearing within the competent carbonate platform, overthrust of the turbidites onto the carbonate platform along the lithological contacts, and WNW-trending thrusts and related asymmetric tight folds in incompetent turbidites. Kinematic analyses revealed that northeast-southwest to north northeast–south southwest regional shortening occurred. The U-Pb dating of the synkinematic calcite constrains the D1 deformation age of 240.5 ± 5.2 Ma. Syn-ore D2 deformation is associated with north-south shortening and features NE-striking sinistral and NW-striking dextral strike-slip mineralized faults that cut through the D1 structures. Gold orebodies formed in the stratigraphic-structural traps, like tight folds, thrusts, and strike-slip faults in turbidites at the less compressed and permeable eastern and western edges of the platform. Gold mineralization in those edges is mainly invisible gold in the rims of zoned pyrite and arsenopyrite in quartz veins, hydraulic breccia, and altered turbidites with ore-related silicification, sulfidation, sericitization, and minor carbonatization. The inferred stress field and published bitumen Re-Os age suggest that the D1 and D2 deformations were formed during Triassic inversion and record a change in direction of the propagation process of basin inversion. The post-ore D3-1 deformation is characterized by west-northwest–east-southeast shortening, whereas the latest post-ore D3-2 deformation represents extension. The north-northeastward basin inversion progressively propagated from the southernmost toward the northernmost of the Youjiang basin during the Middle to Late Triassic. This inversion involved horizontal crustal shortening and vertical thickening, leading to overpressure of deep fluids and triggering large-scale vertical fluid migration via regional inverted preexisting faults. These faults served as conduits, linking the deep source with shallower traps in the fold-and-thrust belt. At shallow depths, gold deposition during compression is controlled by lithological and rheological contacts along competent isolated platforms, which provide better permeability and Fe-bearing reactants that facilitate the precipitation of gold-bearing sulfide. The geochronology of progressive basin evolution and regional sediment-hosted gold mineralization reveals a coupled spatiotemporal relationship between them. Both the sedimentary facies and structural architecture preparation during basin formation and the following structural inversion and lithological-rheological conditions during basin inversion jointly controlled gold deposition. The identified processes for the formation of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits can be used to guide mineral exploration in inverted sedimentary basins worldwide. Published: 2026-07-08T19:37:57+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.595 (consider)

Abstract The Youjiang basin underwent late Paleozoic expansion and Triassic inversion, resulting in scattered Paleozoic isolated carbonate platforms surrounded by Triassic turbidites. The Gaolong gold deposit is representative of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits formed along isolated platforms in the central-southern basin, where three stages of deformation were identified. The pre-ore D1 deformation in Gaolong features a WNW-trending brachy-anticline and NNE-trending dextral strike-slip shearing within the competent carbonate platform, overthrust of the turbidites onto the carbonate platform along the lithological contacts, and WNW-trending thrusts and related asymmetric tight folds in incompetent turbidites. Kinematic analyses revealed that northeast-southwest to north northeast–south southwest regional shortening occurred. The U-Pb dating of the synkinematic calcite constrains the D1 deformation age of 240.5 ± 5.2 Ma. Syn-ore D2 deformation is associated with north-south shortening and features NE-striking sinistral and NW-striking dextral strike-slip mineralized faults that cut through the D1 structures. Gold orebodies formed in the stratigraphic-structural traps, like tight folds, thrusts, and strike-slip faults in turbidites at the less compressed and permeable eastern and western edges of the platform. Gold mineralization in those edges is mainly invisible gold in the rims of zoned pyrite and arsenopyrite in quartz veins, hydraulic breccia, and altered turbidites with ore-related silicification, sulfidation, sericitization, and minor carbonatization. The inferred stress field and published bitumen Re-Os age suggest that the D1 and D2 deformations were formed during Triassic inversion and record a change in direction of the propagation process of basin inversion. The post-ore D3-1 deformation is characterized by west-northwest–east-southeast shortening, whereas the latest post-ore D3-2 deformation represents extension. The north-northeastward basin inversion progressively propagated from the southernmost toward the northernmost of the Youjiang basin during the Middle to Late Triassic. This inversion involved horizontal crustal shortening and vertical thickening, leading to overpressure of deep fluids and triggering large-scale vertical fluid migration via regional inverted preexisting faults. item.title: Interface Deformation, Exhumation, and Underplating: The Role of Plate Velocity Changes Throughout the Evolution of a Subduction Zone
item.dc:creator: Valeria Turino
Adam F. Holt
Derek J. Neuharth
Ryan K. Stoner
Whitney M. Behr
item.description: Abstract Exhumed metamorphic rocks yield critical insights into the mechanical, chemical, and thermal structure of the plate interface that can be used to infer exhumation pathways and their evolution throughout a subduction zone's lifetime. Metamorphosed oceanic rocks often record exhumation and underplating over relatively punctuated time periods, which appear to be linked to specific stages of subduction. Although plate velocities also vary from one stage of subduction to the next, the influence of this time dependence on exhumation and underplating remains unclear. We therefore use 2‐D time‐dependent subduction models to investigate how evolving plate velocities affect the timing and distribution of exhumation and underplating. By extracting plate velocities from a freely evolving subduction model and imposing them on kinematically driven models, we isolate the effects of realistic velocity variations on interface processes without the complexity of a dynamically evolving slab. We also explore the role of interface strength by varying its rheology and composition. In our models, exhumation consistently initiates during a punctuated detachment event following decreases in subduction velocity. Shallow exhumation ( Published: 2026-07-03T18:01:01+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.592 (consider)

item.title: Tracing hydrothermal fluid evolution of the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit (Eastern China) using integrated infrared spectroscopic characteristics and magnetic susceptibility
item.dc:creator: Shili Peng
Shitao Zhang
Hao Xu
Gaobin Chu
Longfei He
Yu Zhang
Hua Zhang
Rucao Li
Yihui Lyu
Di Ding
Siyuan Zhang
Haisen Huang
item.description: The Middle–Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt (MLYRB) is one of the dominant skarn–porphyry Cu–Au metallogenic belts in Eastern China. Within this belt, skarn–porphyry Cu–Au deposits show a close genetic linkage to Late Mesozoic high‑K calc‑alkaline granitoids, and are associated with variable degrees of critical metals mineralization (i.e., Co, Te, Se and Re). Previous studies have generally suggested that these granitoids possess limited W metallogenic potential; nevertheless, several concealed medium‑to‑large W–Cu–Mo deposits have been discovered in their peripheral areas in recent years. At present, the hydrothermal fluid evolution of these W‑rich skarn–porphyry Cu polymetallic deposits remains poorly constrained, and their deep exploration indicators still need further optimization. This study focuses on the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit in the westernmost segment of the MLYRB. The deposit hosts contained metal resources of 0.55 Mt Cu, 0.01 Mt Mo, and 0.012 Mt WO3, with ore grades of 0.86 wt%, 0.10 wt%, and 0.19 wt%, respectively. By combining detailed petrographic observations with short-wave infrared (SWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) spectral alteration mapping, this study pinpoints the precise ore-forming stages and delineates detailed alteration zoning features in the Tongshankou Cu–Mo–W deposit. Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). Notably, the presence of kaolinite and dickite along the intrusive contact zones between ore-forming porphyries and carbonate wall rocks provides direct evidence for acidic fluid activity during the Cu–Mo–W mineralization process. The development of acid fluid conditions in the system is tightly linked to hydrogen ion release during the precipitation of iron oxides and sulfide minerals. Collectively, these results reveal that hydrothermal fluids associated with Cu–Mo–W mineralization in the Tongshankou deposit evolved from alkaline to acidic throughout the mineralization process. Combined with previous research advances, the present findings further suggest that acidic hydrothermal fluids likely represent a ubiquitous component of skarn-hosted Cu–Mo–W mineralization systems. Clay indicator minerals, including kaolinite and dickite, provide robust constraints on the hydrothermal fluid evolution of Tongshankou deposit and serve as newly recognized, effective exploration proxies for analogous skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposits worldwide. Significantly, this study demonstrates that the integrated application of SWIR and TIR spectroscopic analyses combined with magnetic susceptibility measurements provides a rigorous and reliable approach for identifying concealed skarn–porphyry ore bodies across the MLYRB, Eastern China. Published: 2026-07-05T22:00:24+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.591 (consider)

Within this belt, skarn–porphyry Cu–Au deposits show a close genetic linkage to Late Mesozoic high‑K calc‑alkaline granitoids, and are associated with variable degrees of critical metals mineralization (i.e., Co, Te, Se and Re). Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). item.title: Reassessing long-term exhumation rates in magmatic terranes
item.dc:creator: F.J. Cooper
B.A. Adams
S.I.R. Dahlström
T.A. Ehlers
M.C. van Soest
K.V. Hodges
B.R. Jicha
B.S. Singer
J. Cortes Yañez
R.J. Perkins
item.description: Crustal exhumation is central to mountain building, weathering, and sediment production processes, which significantly influence the composition and behavior of Earth’s oceans and atmosphere. It also controls the formation, enrichment, and preservation of porphyry copper deposits, which are a vital source of metals for the clean energy technologies underpinning the global green energy transition. Exhumation rates are typically determined from rock cooling histories combined with a geothermal model. However, in magmatically active regions, models can overpredict exhumation rates because the elevated heat signature is mistakenly attributed to advection driven by rapid exhumation. We present a method to accurately constrain exhumation histories in magmatic terranes that combines thermokinematic modeling of thermochronometric cooling histories with independent geobarometric constraints on the total exhumed crustal thickness. Focusing on the Central Andes, which has a prolonged history of magmatic and magmatic-hydrothermal activity, we find that accounting for this additional source of heat results in exhumed crustal thicknesses within error of the independent constraints, whereas ignoring it results in exhumed thicknesses up to four times greater than geologically plausible. Published: 2026-07-07T04:39:43+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.591 (consider)

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120217

Crustal exhumation is central to mountain building, weathering, and sediment production processes, which significantly influence the composition and behavior of Earth’s oceans and atmosphere. It also controls the formation, enrichment, and preservation of porphyry copper deposits, which are a vital source of metals for the clean energy technologies underpinning the global green energy transition. However, in magmatically active regions, models can overpredict exhumation rates because the elevated heat signature is mistakenly attributed to advection driven by rapid exhumation. Focusing on the Central Andes, which has a prolonged history of magmatic and magmatic-hydrothermal activity, we find that accounting for this additional source of heat results in exhumed crustal thicknesses within error of the independent constraints, whereas ignoring it results in exhumed thicknesses up to four times greater than geologically plausible.

item.title: Structure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins
item.dc:creator: Yong-Fei Zheng
Yi-Xiang Chen
Li-Qun Dai
Wei Dan
Sanzhong Li
item.description: Plate margins are the dynamic boundaries where lithospheric plates interact, driving Earth’s tectonic evolution. While the asthenospheric material and energy are transported along divergent plate margins to the Earth’s surface, the surface material is transferred along convergent plate margins into the Earth’s interior. Therefore, plate divergence and convergence are two key mechanisms for the exchange of material and energy between the lithosphere and asthenosphere. A great deal of advances in understanding thestructure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins have been made in the past two decades. Divergent margins are manifested as seafloor spreading and continental rift zones, and they occur as mid-ocean ridges, successful and failed continental rifts in different phases. Convergent margins are manifested as oceanic subduction and continental collision zones, and their formation and evolution are dictated by the properties of converging plates. Therefore, they record a series of processes for the Wilson cycle in plate tectonics. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes of plate margins are two critical variables in modulating the internal and external Earth systems. In general, the geothermal gradient of plate margins changes from cold through warm to hot during the transition from convergence to divergence, and it correlates with the dynamic regime of either horizontal or vertical forces via specific tectonic processes. Cold subduction proceeds at low geothermal gradients, producing low T/P Alpine-type blueschist to eclogite facies metamorphic rocks; warm collision proceeds at mediate geothermal gradients, leading to medium T/P Barrovian-type amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks; and hot rifting proceeds at high geothermal gradients, generating high T/P amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks. Oceanic plates may subduct either actively or passively beneath continental margins, giving rise to different products at the overriding continental margins. Active subduction results in accretionary wedge, andesitic arc magmatism and porphyry mineralization, which are absent in the case of passive subduction. Continental assembly progresses from soft collision through hard collision to deep subduction, producing different types of regional metamorphism along convergent plate margins. Syn-collisional orogeny during continental assembly is characterized by medium T/P Barrovian-type metamorphism in the compressional regime, whereas post-collisional orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. Such spatiotemporal relationships between the dynamic and thermobaric regimes are definite at plate margins. Magmatism always proceeds at high geothermal gradients and thus is associated with extensional regimes either in the late stage of oceanic subduction or in the post-collisional stage. In this regard, caution must be exercised in linking HT/LP metamorphism and felsic magmatism to continental collision and supercontinental assembly. The formation and evolution of intracontinental tectonics have the inheritance and development relationships to the structure and composition of precursor plate margins. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes are two key variables in dictating the physicochemical properties and geodynamic processes of continental interiors. Thethermo-mechanical coupling is significant at the plate margins, where feedback between thermobaric regimes (i.e., geothermal gradients) and dynamic forces (i.e., buoyancy vs gravity, and compression vs extension) governs a series of geological processes such as deformation, metamorphism and magmatism. Therefore, plate tectonics operate viafeedback loopswhere thermobaric anomalies (e.g., asthenospheric heating vs slab cooling) modify rock rheology, inducing these geological processes that reshapes the architecture of active and fossil plate margins. As a result, continental rifting along previously convergent plate margins plays a critical role not only in physical and chemical reworking of the plate margins but also in initiating new Wilson cycles. As such, intracontinental orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. The relevant elements are the important components of plate tectonics theory and thus have significant linkages to resources, energy and disasters. Published: 2026-07-09T12:55:31+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.588 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yong-Fei Zheng; Yi-Xiang Chen; Li-Qun Dai; Wei Dan; Sanzhong Li

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102403

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

Plate margins are the dynamic boundaries where lithospheric plates interact, driving Earth’s tectonic evolution. While the asthenospheric material and energy are transported along divergent plate margins to the Earth’s surface, the surface material is transferred along convergent plate margins into the Earth’s interior. Therefore, plate divergence and convergence are two key mechanisms for the exchange of material and energy between the lithosphere and asthenosphere. A great deal of advances in understanding thestructure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins have been made in the past two decades. Divergent margins are manifested as seafloor spreading and continental rift zones, and they occur as mid-ocean ridges, successful and failed continental rifts in different phases. Convergent margins are manifested as oceanic subduction and continental collision zones, and their formation and evolution are dictated by the properties of converging plates. Therefore, they record a series of processes for the Wilson cycle in plate tectonics. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes of plate margins are two critical variables in modulating the internal and external Earth systems. In general, the geothermal gradient of plate margins changes from cold through warm to hot during the transition from convergence to divergence, and it correlates with the dynamic regime of either horizontal or vertical forces via specific tectonic processes. Cold subduction proceeds at low geothermal gradients, producing low T/P Alpine-type blueschist to eclogite facies metamorphic rocks; warm collision proceeds at mediate geothermal gradients, leading to medium T/P Barrovian-type amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks; and hot rifting proceeds at high geothermal gradients, generating high T/P amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks. Oceanic plates may subduct either actively or passively beneath continental margins, giving rise to different products at the overriding continental margins. Active subduction results in accretionary wedge, andesitic arc magmatism and porphyry mineralization, which are absent in the case of passive subduction. Continental assembly progresses from soft collision through hard collision to deep subduction, producing different types of regional metamorphism along convergent plate margins. Syn-collisional orogeny during continental assembly is characterized by medium T/P Barrovian-type metamorphism in the compressional regime, whereas post-collisional orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. Such spatiotemporal relationships between the dynamic and thermobaric regimes are definite at plate margins. Magmatism always proceeds at high geothermal gradients and thus is associated with extensional regimes either in the late stage of oceanic subduction or in the post-collisional stage. In this regard, caution must be exercised in linking HT/LP metamorphism and felsic magmatism to continental collision and supercontinental assembly. The formation and evolution of intracontinental tectonics have the inheritance and development relationships to the structure and composition of precursor plate margins. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes are two key variables in dictating the physicochemical properties and geodynamic processes of continental interiors. Thethermo-mechanical coupling is significant at the plate margins, where feedback between thermobaric regimes (i.e., geothermal gradients) and dynamic forces (i.e., buoyancy vs gravity, and compression vs extension) governs a series of geological processes such as deformation, metamorphism and magmatism. Therefore, plate tectonics operate viafeedback loopswhere thermobaric anomalies (e.g., asthenospheric heating vs slab cooling) modify rock rheology, inducing these geological processes that reshapes the architecture of active and fossil plate margins. As a result, continental rifting along previously convergent plate margins plays a critical role not only in physical and chemical reworking of the plate margins but also in initiating new Wilson cycles. As such, intracontinental orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. The relevant elements are the important components of plate tectonics theory and thus have significant linkages to resources, energy and disasters.

item.title: Geochronology and genesis of rare-metal mineralization in the Himalayan leucogranite-pegmatite system
item.dc:creator: Menglan Ran
Xiaoming Sun
Ying Liu
Zhengpeng Ding
Guangming Li
item.description: Himalayan leucogranites are a key target for rare-metal exploration, yet the sources and petrogenetic evolution of their parental magmas remain debated. This study investigates three representative deposits hosted by leucogranite-pegmatite systems in distinct tectonic units of the Himalayan orogen, integrating apatite and cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock and apatite geochemistry, and apatite oxygen isotopes. Combined cassiterite and apatite dating reveals that rare-metal mineralization developed through multiple discrete magmatic-hydrothermal pulses rather than a single event, defining a prolonged, multi-stage metallogenic framework. Whole-rock and apatite trace-element signatures indicate highly fractionated, relatively oxidized melts that experienced extensive fluid exsolution, consistent with derivation from ancient metasedimentary sources within the Higher Himalayan Crystalline Sequence. These pegmatites likely evolved from parental leucogranitic magmas through extreme fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Apatite oxygen isotopes further resolve three contrasting fluid-evolution paths among the deposits. These range from open-system overprinting by external fluids, through continuous internal magmatic-to-hydrothermal evolution, to a largely closed system dominated by extreme fractionation, with each path corresponding to a distinct mineralization style. Collectively, these data suggest that post-collisional crustal decompression promoted partial melting of metasedimentary rocks, and the resultant leucogranitic magmas underwent high-degree fractionation and fluid exsolution to form rare-metal-bearing pegmatites. These findings constrain crustal melting, magmatic differentiation, and rare-metal enrichment in the Himalayan collisional orogen and highlight apatite as a sensitive tracer of pegmatite-forming processes. Published: 2026-07-03T06:44:11+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.588 (consider)

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108702

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

Himalayan leucogranites are a key target for rare-metal exploration, yet the sources and petrogenetic evolution of their parental magmas remain debated. This study investigates three representative deposits hosted by leucogranite-pegmatite systems in distinct tectonic units of the Himalayan orogen, integrating apatite and cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock and apatite geochemistry, and apatite oxygen isotopes. Combined cassiterite and apatite dating reveals that rare-metal mineralization developed through multiple discrete magmatic-hydrothermal pulses rather than a single event, defining a prolonged, multi-stage metallogenic framework. Whole-rock and apatite trace-element signatures indicate highly fractionated, relatively oxidized melts that experienced extensive fluid exsolution, consistent with derivation from ancient metasedimentary sources within the Higher Himalayan Crystalline Sequence. These pegmatites likely evolved from parental leucogranitic magmas through extreme fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Apatite oxygen isotopes further resolve three contrasting fluid-evolution paths among the deposits. These range from open-system overprinting by external fluids, through continuous internal magmatic-to-hydrothermal evolution, to a largely closed system dominated by extreme fractionation, with each path corresponding to a distinct mineralization style. Collectively, these data suggest that post-collisional crustal decompression promoted partial melting of metasedimentary rocks, and the resultant leucogranitic magmas underwent high-degree fractionation and fluid exsolution to form rare-metal-bearing pegmatites. These findings constrain crustal melting, magmatic differentiation, and rare-metal enrichment in the Himalayan collisional orogen and highlight apatite as a sensitive tracer of pegmatite-forming processes.

item.title: Fingerprints of s-cl-bearing fluids deduced from metal and mineral microinclusions in Quaternary Kamchatka lavas
item.dc:creator: Pavel Kepezhinskas
Nikolai Berdnikov
Nadezhda Potapova
Valeria Krutikova
item.description: Quaternary ankaramite, basalt, andesite and dacite from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes in Kamchatka contain numerous mineral microinclusions in rock-forming silicate and accessory oxide minerals as well as in groundmass volcanic glass. We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. S-Cl-bearing microinclusions as Ag (± Cu) chlorides, composite S-O-C-bearing metal chlorides, Ag (±Cu) sulfides, sulfides of Fe, Ni, Cu, Pb, Zn and Sb as well as barite, cassiterite and apatite are very characteristic for these rocks. Primary-textured, magmatic microinclusions in olivine, pyroxenes, amphibole, plagioclase and fresh volcanic glass show no evidence for the later-stage transformations. Secondary-textured, post-magmatic microinclusions are localized in pores, voids and fractures or along the boundaries of silicate minerals and in association with secondary minerals. Magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing microinclusion assemblages almost completely overlap compositionally suggesting that S-Cl-bearing fluids accompanied formation of Kamchatka lavas from magmatic differentiation in sub-volcanic conduits to their emplacement, cooling and degassing above the surface as well as possible post-eruptive transformations near active fumarolic fields. Lavas from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes display elevated Pb, Zn, Sb, Cu and Ag concentrations in comparison with the average MORB and upper continental crust. Some volcanic samples with both magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing mineral microinclusions have higher Mo, Sn, Sb and Ag contents than previously reported for Kamchatka volcanoes suggesting that sulfur and chlorine potentially contribute to metal enrichment in magmas and associated fluids and facilitate transport of certain ore metals in the Kamchatka subduction zone. Published: 2026-07-04T15:23:16+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.588 (consider)

Quaternary ankaramite, basalt, andesite and dacite from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes in Kamchatka contain numerous mineral microinclusions in rock-forming silicate and accessory oxide minerals as well as in groundmass volcanic glass. We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. Lavas from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes display elevated Pb, Zn, Sb, Cu and Ag concentrations in comparison with the average MORB and upper continental crust.
