item.title: Natural CO2 sequestration: mechanisms and conditions of carbonate precipitation in basalt
item.dc:creator: M.P. Rosenqvist
K.G. Dunkel
L.P. Nogueira
S. Planke
S. Polteau
M. Ardit
F. Nestola
L. Menegon
item.description: Carbon sequestration in mafic and ultramafic rocks has been demonstrated as a safe and efficient storage method; however, the specifics of the carbonation reactions remain uncertain, especially in volcanic reservoirs. In this study, we contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms and conditions of natural CO2 sequestration in basalt by analysing 43 early Eocene basalt samples from IODP boreholes U1571A and U1572A on the Skoll High on the mid-Norwegian continental margin. Using high-resolution 2D and 3D imaging and microanalytical techniques together with X-ray powder diffraction, U-Pb dating, as well as carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, we identified two main types of carbonate precipitation: 1) direct precipitation in vesicles, and 2) replacement of olivine and clinopyroxene by coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. While most vesicles are coated with smectite, this did not inhibit fluid flow and carbonate precipitation. Mineral parageneses, δ13C ratios, and δ18O ratios record carbonate precipitation in the vesicles at < 100˚C and suggest a variation of carbon sources, from early intruded meteoric water mixed with volcanic and thermogenic carbon to marine sourced carbon. Vesicle carbonate comprises up to 8.6 vol% of its host basalt (as determined by μ-CT image analysis) and replacement carbonate 0.6 – 0.94 wt% (as determined by powder diffraction). We estimate that within the upper 100 m of the subaerially emplaced basalt sequence on Skoll High, approximately 1 Mt of CO2 is stored as carbonate per km2 area, at least 0.3 Mt of which has precipitated in the microporous basalt through coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. This illustrates that the storage potential of basaltic rocks is not limited to the macro-porosity (vesicles) but may be significantly enhanced by replacement reactions. Published: 2026-07-09T12:39:23+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M.P. Rosenqvist; K.G. Dunkel; L.P. Nogueira; S. Planke; S. Polteau; M. Ardit; F. Nestola; L. Menegon

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.013

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

Carbon sequestration in mafic and ultramafic rocks has been demonstrated as a safe and efficient storage method; however, the specifics of the carbonation reactions remain uncertain, especially in volcanic reservoirs. In this study, we contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms and conditions of natural CO2 sequestration in basalt by analysing 43 early Eocene basalt samples from IODP boreholes U1571A and U1572A on the Skoll High on the mid-Norwegian continental margin. Using high-resolution 2D and 3D imaging and microanalytical techniques together with X-ray powder diffraction, U-Pb dating, as well as carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, we identified two main types of carbonate precipitation: 1) direct precipitation in vesicles, and 2) replacement of olivine and clinopyroxene by coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. While most vesicles are coated with smectite, this did not inhibit fluid flow and carbonate precipitation. Mineral parageneses, δ13C ratios, and δ18O ratios record carbonate precipitation in the vesicles at < 100˚C and suggest a variation of carbon sources, from early intruded meteoric water mixed with volcanic and thermogenic carbon to marine sourced carbon. Vesicle carbonate comprises up to 8.6 vol% of its host basalt (as determined by μ-CT image analysis) and replacement carbonate 0.6 – 0.94 wt% (as determined by powder diffraction). We estimate that within the upper 100 m of the subaerially emplaced basalt sequence on Skoll High, approximately 1 Mt of CO2 is stored as carbonate per km2 area, at least 0.3 Mt of which has precipitated in the microporous basalt through coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. This illustrates that the storage potential of basaltic rocks is not limited to the macro-porosity (vesicles) but may be significantly enhanced by replacement reactions.

item.title: Geochemical, mineralogical, and geochronological constraints on the Wugongshan granite-aplite complex: Implications for regional Li-Nb-Ta mineralization in South China
item.dc:creator: Xiaodong Ma
Bin Wu
Yinqiu Hu
Guanglai Li
Bo Peng
Kai Xu
Yuehu Lyu
item.description: A key obstacle to understanding rare-metal mineralization in highly evolved granitic systems is the uncertain petrogenetic relationship between granites and their felsic derivatives. This study presents an integrated investigation of Mesozoic granites and aplite dikes from the Wugongshan complex in South China, which incorporates whole-rock geochemistry, mineral composition, U-Pb dating of apatite and cassiterite, and apatite Sr isotope. Both rock types crystallized at ∼ 151–150 Ma. Relative to barren granites, the Li-Nb-Ta mineralized aplite are characterized by the crystallization of lepidolite, columbite-(Mn), tantalite-(Mn), and microlite. Geochemical and mineralogical evidence indicates that the aplite crystallized from a more evolved, fluid-rich melts during the magmatic-hydrothermal transition stage. However, mineralogical and Sr isotopic discrepancies between the granites (whole-rock initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7143–0.7208, from literature) and the aplite (apatite initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7512–0.7648) preclude a simple in situ differentiation origin for the aplite from the exposed granites. Instead, it probably originated from a more reduced, isotopically and compositionally heterogeneous underlying batch, either derived from a different deep-seated magma chamber or from the same chamber that sourced the exposed granites but subsequently underwent crustal assimilation at depth. Both alternative models are characterized by high initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios. Regional comparisons with Yichun and Yifeng deposits imply that rare-metal granite-aplite systems in western Jiangxi may result from multiple episodes of granitic magmatism, reflecting heterogeneous source melting and multistage magma recharge on a regional scale, rather than being sole products of continuous fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Published: 2026-07-03T23:07:49+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.585 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaodong Ma; Bin Wu; Yinqiu Hu; Guanglai Li; Bo Peng; Kai Xu; Yuehu Lyu

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107422

Relevance: 0.585 (consider)

A key obstacle to understanding rare-metal mineralization in highly evolved granitic systems is the uncertain petrogenetic relationship between granites and their felsic derivatives. This study presents an integrated investigation of Mesozoic granites and aplite dikes from the Wugongshan complex in South China, which incorporates whole-rock geochemistry, mineral composition, U-Pb dating of apatite and cassiterite, and apatite Sr isotope. Both rock types crystallized at ∼ 151–150 Ma. Relative to barren granites, the Li-Nb-Ta mineralized aplite are characterized by the crystallization of lepidolite, columbite-(Mn), tantalite-(Mn), and microlite. Geochemical and mineralogical evidence indicates that the aplite crystallized from a more evolved, fluid-rich melts during the magmatic-hydrothermal transition stage. However, mineralogical and Sr isotopic discrepancies between the granites (whole-rock initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7143–0.7208, from literature) and the aplite (apatite initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7512–0.7648) preclude a simple in situ differentiation origin for the aplite from the exposed granites. Instead, it probably originated from a more reduced, isotopically and compositionally heterogeneous underlying batch, either derived from a different deep-seated magma chamber or from the same chamber that sourced the exposed granites but subsequently underwent crustal assimilation at depth. Both alternative models are characterized by high initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios. Regional comparisons with Yichun and Yifeng deposits imply that rare-metal granite-aplite systems in western Jiangxi may result from multiple episodes of granitic magmatism, reflecting heterogeneous source melting and multistage magma recharge on a regional scale, rather than being sole products of continuous fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution.

item.title: Petrogenesis of mafic microgranular enclaves in the Lingshan granitic intrusion, eastern South China: new insights from X-ray CT analysis and macro elemental mapping
item.dc:creator: Yun-Hao Sun
Kong-Yang Zhu
Lin-Lin She
Feng-Qi Zhang
Yi-Xian Xu
Chuan-Wan Dong
Zhong-Yue Shen
item.description: This study investigates mafic microgranular enclaves (MMEs) within the Lingshan granite (Jiangxi Province, South China) through an integrated approach combining traditional geochemistry, macro-X-ray fluorescence (MA-XRF) mapping, and X-ray computed tomography (X-CT). Zircon U-Pb ages and Hf isotopes indicate that the MMEs share similar crystallization ages and isotopic ratio with their host granite. X-CT imaging reveals similar short-axis and long-axis mineral orientations (biotite, hornblende, magnetite, ilmenite and some heavy minerals) between MMEs and host rock, supporting flow alignment of mafic magma globules within the felsic melt (globules of a more mafic magma injected into the felsic magma and subsequently mingled and mixed with it) rather than a restite, or early cumulate origin (the mineral orientations of MMEs in these models are nearly impossible to be consistent with those of the host granite.). Whole-rock geochemistry exhibits non-linear trace element trends, which are inconsistent with simple magma mixing but rather an example of the chaotic nature of the mixing process. MA-XRF mapping further demonstrates enrichment of Fe, Mn, Ti, Y, Nb, and Ca alongside depletion of Sr, K, and Rb in MMEs, attributed to diffusion-driven exchange. The hybridization process weakened the A-type signature of the host granite, implying that some I-type granites may derive from A-type precursors, primarily through magma mixing. Notably, MMEs may sequester some critical ore-forming elements (e.g., Nb, Ta), potentially suppressing the metallogenic potential of the host granite. These findings may provide new constraints on magma differentiation processes and related mineralization mechanisms in South China's granitic systems. Published: 2026-07-09T09:26:53+00:00 Venue: Journal of the Geological Society Score: 0.584 (consider)

Authors: Yun-Hao Sun; Kong-Yang Zhu; Lin-Lin She; Feng-Qi Zhang; Yi-Xian Xu; Chuan-Wan Dong; Zhong-Yue Shen

Journal: Journal of the Geological Society

DOI: 10.1144/jgs2025-093

Relevance: 0.584 (consider)

This study investigates mafic microgranular enclaves (MMEs) within the Lingshan granite (Jiangxi Province, South China) through an integrated approach combining traditional geochemistry, macro-X-ray fluorescence (MA-XRF) mapping, and X-ray computed tomography (X-CT). Zircon U-Pb ages and Hf isotopes indicate that the MMEs share similar crystallization ages and isotopic ratio with their host granite. X-CT imaging reveals similar short-axis and long-axis mineral orientations (biotite, hornblende, magnetite, ilmenite and some heavy minerals) between MMEs and host rock, supporting flow alignment of mafic magma globules within the felsic melt (globules of a more mafic magma injected into the felsic magma and subsequently mingled and mixed with it) rather than a restite, or early cumulate origin (the mineral orientations of MMEs in these models are nearly impossible to be consistent with those of the host granite.). Whole-rock geochemistry exhibits non-linear trace element trends, which are inconsistent with simple magma mixing but rather an example of the chaotic nature of the mixing process. MA-XRF mapping further demonstrates enrichment of Fe, Mn, Ti, Y, Nb, and Ca alongside depletion of Sr, K, and Rb in MMEs, attributed to diffusion-driven exchange. The hybridization process weakened the A-type signature of the host granite, implying that some I-type granites may derive from A-type precursors, primarily through magma mixing. Notably, MMEs may sequester some critical ore-forming elements (e.g., Nb, Ta), potentially suppressing the metallogenic potential of the host granite. These findings may provide new constraints on magma differentiation processes and related mineralization mechanisms in South China's granitic systems.

item.title: Regional-scale integrated 3D modeling of the northern Norrbotten ore province (Sweden): Implications on the iron oxide-apatite mineral system
item.dc:creator: Ervin Veress
Oskar Rydman
Tobias E. Bauer
Joel BH. Andersson
Maxim Y. Smirnov
Thorkild M. Rasmussen
Irvine R. Annesley
item.description: The northern Norrbotten ore province, situated within the Fennoscandian Shield, constitutes a major metallogenic domain in Europe and hosts more than 40 iron oxide-apatite (IOA) deposits, including the Kiirunavaara deposit, the type locality for this deposit type. Although extensive mapping, geochemical, geochronological, and structural studies have refined the conceptual understanding of IOA systems at deposit and district scales, a fully integrated three-dimensional framework that synthesizes this knowledge with regional geological and geophysical datasets has not yet been developed. This study presents an integrated 3D model of the northern Norrbotten ore province that incorporates lithological, structural, and petrophysical constraints with inversions of magnetic and gravity data, as well as available magnetotelluric conductivity models. The geological models delineate the architecture of intrusive suites, supracrustal belts, crustal-scale shear zones, and subordinate brittle–ductile second-order structures, implemented in a voxel-based domain for quantitative spatial integration. Mafic to ultramafic intrusions previously linked locally to IOA genesis are consistently coincident with high-density, magnetically anomalous domains and spatially associated with vertically extensive electrical conductors. These features are interpreted as the upper-crustal expression of a deeper transcrustal conduit system that facilitated magma ascent, fluid migration, and heat transfer. IOA deposits are located within second-order oblique structures intersecting or splaying from first-order structures, and their geometry and extent correlate with diagnostic magnetic signatures characterized by steep gradients, localized highs, and adjacent negative side lobes. These findings refine the metallogenic framework for IOA mineralization in the northern Norrbotten ore province and provide a robust 3D geoscientific basis for testing mineral system hypotheses. Published: 2026-07-04T22:23:15+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.583 (consider)

The northern Norrbotten ore province, situated within the Fennoscandian Shield, constitutes a major metallogenic domain in Europe and hosts more than 40 iron oxide-apatite (IOA) deposits, including the Kiirunavaara deposit, the type locality for this deposit type. Although extensive mapping, geochemical, geochronological, and structural studies have refined the conceptual understanding of IOA systems at deposit and district scales, a fully integrated three-dimensional framework that synthesizes this knowledge with regional geological and geophysical datasets has not yet been developed. This study presents an integrated 3D model of the northern Norrbotten ore province that incorporates lithological, structural, and petrophysical constraints with inversions of magnetic and gravity data, as well as available magnetotelluric conductivity models. The geological models delineate the architecture of intrusive suites, supracrustal belts, crustal-scale shear zones, and subordinate brittle–ductile second-order structures, implemented in a voxel-based domain for quantitative spatial integration. Mafic to ultramafic intrusions previously linked locally to IOA genesis are consistently coincident with high-density, magnetically anomalous domains and spatially associated with vertically extensive electrical conductors. IOA deposits are located within second-order oblique structures intersecting or splaying from first-order structures, and their geometry and extent correlate with diagnostic magnetic signatures characterized by steep gradients, localized highs, and adjacent negative side lobes. These findings refine the metallogenic framework for IOA mineralization in the northern Norrbotten ore province and provide a robust 3D geoscientific basis for testing mineral system hypotheses.

item.title: Vanadium isotopes demonstrate depth-dependent magma differentiation in Rindjani Volcano, Sunda arc
item.dc:creator: Xin Ding
Min Ji
Weixin Lv
Fang Huang
Erik V. Sørensen
Paul M. Holm
John D. Foden
item.description: The andesitic composition of the continental crust—characterized by high SiO2 and low FeOT contents—has been attributed to differentiation processes operating in arc settings. One contributing mechanism could be amphibole-forming peritectic reaction at the crust–mantle boundary; however, its role in arc magma differentiation remains poorly constrained. Here we present high-precision δ 51/50V data (hereafter δ51V) for volcanic rocks from the Rindjani suite in the Sunda arc that span compositions from high-Al basalts to trachydacites, investigating how V isotopes respond to amphibole-controlled magma differentiation. At the crust–mantle boundary, amphibole-forming peritectic reactions transform H2O-rich magma into Si-rich melt and induce minor δ51V variation of approximately 0.08‰. H2O-rich magmas that bypass the peritectic reaction ascend to the shallow crust and differentiate from high-Al basalts to high-K andesites, showing limited δ51V variation from −0.76‰ to −0.66‰. Shallow-level differentiation of the Si-rich melt from andesites to trachydacites produces a sharp increase in δ51V from −0.62‰ to −0.04‰, consistent with an average mineral-melt fractionation factor Δ51Vmineral–melt ≈ −0.30‰. Published Fe−Ti isotope data show that δ56Fe remained constant, whereas δ49Ti responded to differentiation throughout the compositional range of the Rindjani suite. The decoupled Fe–Ti–V isotope systematics reveal differences in elemental mass balance and mineral–melt isotope fractionation during magma differentiation. These results show that amphibole-forming peritectic reactions fundamentally modify melt chemistry, thereby enabling significant V isotope fractionation associated with magnetite crystallization at shallow levels. Our findings highlight the depth–dependent differentiation framework of arc magmas and advance understanding of the V isotopic pathways involved in continental crust formation. Published: 2026-07-05T21:55:12+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.582 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xin Ding; Min Ji; Weixin Lv; Fang Huang; Erik V. Sørensen; Paul M. Holm; John D. Foden

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.005

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

The andesitic composition of the continental crust—characterized by high SiO2 and low FeOT contents—has been attributed to differentiation processes operating in arc settings. One contributing mechanism could be amphibole-forming peritectic reaction at the crust–mantle boundary; however, its role in arc magma differentiation remains poorly constrained. Here we present high-precision δ 51/50V data (hereafter δ51V) for volcanic rocks from the Rindjani suite in the Sunda arc that span compositions from high-Al basalts to trachydacites, investigating how V isotopes respond to amphibole-controlled magma differentiation. At the crust–mantle boundary, amphibole-forming peritectic reactions transform H2O-rich magma into Si-rich melt and induce minor δ51V variation of approximately 0.08‰. H2O-rich magmas that bypass the peritectic reaction ascend to the shallow crust and differentiate from high-Al basalts to high-K andesites, showing limited δ51V variation from −0.76‰ to −0.66‰. Shallow-level differentiation of the Si-rich melt from andesites to trachydacites produces a sharp increase in δ51V from −0.62‰ to −0.04‰, consistent with an average mineral-melt fractionation factor Δ51Vmineral–melt ≈ −0.30‰. Published Fe−Ti isotope data show that δ56Fe remained constant, whereas δ49Ti responded to differentiation throughout the compositional range of the Rindjani suite. The decoupled Fe–Ti–V isotope systematics reveal differences in elemental mass balance and mineral–melt isotope fractionation during magma differentiation. These results show that amphibole-forming peritectic reactions fundamentally modify melt chemistry, thereby enabling significant V isotope fractionation associated with magnetite crystallization at shallow levels. Our findings highlight the depth–dependent differentiation framework of arc magmas and advance understanding of the V isotopic pathways involved in continental crust formation.

item.title: Zircon geochronology, geochemistry and bulk-rock Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic characteristics of Neoarchean metamorphic volcanics in the Yinshan Block and tectonic implications
item.dc:creator: Xiaoguang Liu
Yixun Chen
Chen Zhao
Xiao Wang
item.description: The nature of Neoarchean tectonism in the Yinshan Block of the North China Craton (NCC) remains a subject of debate, with competing models proposing either subduction-driven horizontal tectonics or mantle plume-dominated vertical tectonics. This ambiguity largely stems from poorly constrained timing and genetic links between magmatic and metamorphic events. In this study, we present new zircon U-Pb geochronology and Lu-Hf isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic data for metamorphosed volcanic rocks of the Xinghe Group and granitic gneisses from the central Yinshan Block. Our results establish a refined chronological framework revealing three distinct magmatic pulses at ca. 2650 Ma, 2550 Ma, and 2520 Ma, followed by a regional granulite-facies metamorphic event between 2520 Ma and 2500 Ma. Geochemical and isotopic analyses indicate a temporal shift in magma sources. The early Neoarchean (ca. 2650 Ma) volcanic rocks display depleted mantle signatures with low initial 87Sr/86Sr, positive εNd(t), and evident zircon Hf positive values, consistent with derivation from a juvenile mantle source with limited crustal addition. Meanwhile, the geochemical signatures of the 2.8–2.7 Ga zircons reveal a source related to mid-ocean ridge (MOR) or ocean island (OI) settings and intermediate crystallization pressure. In contrast, the late Neoarchean (ca. 2520 Ma) granitoids exhibit variable isotopic compositions and zircon negative zircon εHf(t) excursions, indicative of significant crustal recycling and a mixed source. Integrating these findings, we propose a two-stage geodynamic evolution for the Yinshan Block: an early stage dominated by mantle-derived magmatism (all pre-2.6 Ga magmatic records) and a late stage characterized by intensive crustal thickening and enhanced crust-mantle interaction (2.52–2.50 Ga). This evolutionary pattern mirrors observations in other cratons globally, providing key insights into the geodynamic evolution of the early Earth. Published: 2026-07-09T20:11:55+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.582 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaoguang Liu; Yixun Chen; Chen Zhao; Xiao Wang

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108207

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

The nature of Neoarchean tectonism in the Yinshan Block of the North China Craton (NCC) remains a subject of debate, with competing models proposing either subduction-driven horizontal tectonics or mantle plume-dominated vertical tectonics. This ambiguity largely stems from poorly constrained timing and genetic links between magmatic and metamorphic events. In this study, we present new zircon U-Pb geochronology and Lu-Hf isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic data for metamorphosed volcanic rocks of the Xinghe Group and granitic gneisses from the central Yinshan Block. Our results establish a refined chronological framework revealing three distinct magmatic pulses at ca. 2650 Ma, 2550 Ma, and 2520 Ma, followed by a regional granulite-facies metamorphic event between 2520 Ma and 2500 Ma. Geochemical and isotopic analyses indicate a temporal shift in magma sources. The early Neoarchean (ca. 2650 Ma) volcanic rocks display depleted mantle signatures with low initial 87Sr/86Sr, positive εNd(t), and evident zircon Hf positive values, consistent with derivation from a juvenile mantle source with limited crustal addition. Meanwhile, the geochemical signatures of the 2.8–2.7 Ga zircons reveal a source related to mid-ocean ridge (MOR) or ocean island (OI) settings and intermediate crystallization pressure. In contrast, the late Neoarchean (ca. 2520 Ma) granitoids exhibit variable isotopic compositions and zircon negative zircon εHf(t) excursions, indicative of significant crustal recycling and a mixed source. Integrating these findings, we propose a two-stage geodynamic evolution for the Yinshan Block: an early stage dominated by mantle-derived magmatism (all pre-2.6 Ga magmatic records) and a late stage characterized by intensive crustal thickening and enhanced crust-mantle interaction (2.52–2.50 Ga). This evolutionary pattern mirrors observations in other cratons globally, providing key insights into the geodynamic evolution of the early Earth.
